page.content: In a comprehensive evaluation, Table 1 shows that in the Overall stage, resident comments indicated a "Wonderful experience" with sentiments about advancing more than in previous teaching, emphasizing hands-on learning from patients rather than traditional methods, while SPI comments reflected a successful teaching experience with positive engagement from learners, including observations about non-English speakers. In the Pre-SPI encounter, residents found that prior observation made the SPI experience more effective, aided by online material, while SPI feedback was not applicable. The Pre-SPI lecture/coaching was similarly noted for learning examination techniques. During the SPI session, residents appreciated the SPI's comfort and detailed individualized teaching, encapsulating the importance of understanding patient perspectives and encouraging questions, while SPI noted the session's pacing and the need for modesty. The use of an interpreter was highlighted as novel and complex, facilitating understanding but also adding time. Suggestions for Improvements included the need for training on U.S. speculums and video recordings for self-study. Table 2 presents data with headers including Crude OR, Adjusted OR, 95% CI adjusted OR, Adjusted gain/1000, and 95% CI gain. For the row labeled Illiterate head of household, the Crude OR is 0.59, Adjusted OR is 0.72, 95% CI adjusted OR is 0.61–0.84, Adjusted gain/1000 is 2.4, and 95% CI gain is 1.3–3.5. Waiting time under 20 minutes shows a Crude OR of 2.11, Adjusted OR of 1.81, 95% CI adjusted OR of 1.53–2.14, Adjusted gain/1000 of 61.4, and 95% CI gain of 44.1–78.6. The received all prescribed drugs row reports a Crude OR of 3.73, Adjusted OR of 2.29, 95% CI adjusted OR of 1.84–2.85, Adjusted gain/1000 of 96.3, and 95% CI gain of 70.8–121.8. Additionally, the received explanation about illness section shows a Crude OR of 10.41, Adjusted OR of 2.89, 95% CI adjusted OR of 2.3–3.63, Adjusted gain/1000 of 91.6, and 95% CI gain of 72.1–111.2. The received explanation about remedy row indicates a Crude OR of 10.7, Adjusted OR of 4.61, 95% CI adjusted OR of 3.37–5.69, Adjusted gain/1000 of 154.8, and 95% CI gain of 133.5–176.2. Lastly, the unexplained difference between 2003 and 2000 shows a Crude OR of 1.54, Adjusted OR of 1.81, 95% CI adjusted OR of 1.54–2.11, Adjusted gain/1000 of 34.8, and 95% CI gain of 25.6–44.1.

with sentiments about advancing more than in previous teaching, emphasizing hands-on learning from patients rather than
traditional methods, while SPI comments reflected a successful teaching experience with positive engagement from learners,
including observations about non-English speakers. In the Pre-SPI encounter, residents found that prior observation made the
SPI experience more effective, aided by online material, while SPI feedback was not applicable. The Pre-SPI lecture/coaching

facilitating understanding but also adding time. Suggestions for Improvements included the need for training on U.S. speculums
and video recordings for self-study. Table 2 presents data with headers including Crude OR, Adjusted OR, 95% CI adjusted OR,

shows a Crude OR of 2.11, Adjusted OR of 1.81, 95% CI adjusted OR of 1.53–2.14, Adjusted gain/1000 of 61.4, and 95% CI
gain of 44.1–78.6. The received all prescribed drugs row reports a Crude OR of 3.73, Adjusted OR of 2.29, 95% CI adjusted

and 95% CI gain of 72.1–111.2. The received explanation about remedy row indicates a Crude OR of 10.7, Adjusted OR of
4.61, 95% CI adjusted OR of 3.37–5.69, Adjusted gain/1000 of 154.8, and 95% CI gain of 133.5–176.2. Lastly, the unexplained
difference between 2003 and 2000 shows a Crude OR of 1.54, Adjusted OR of 1.81, 95% CI adjusted OR of 1.54–2.11,
Adjusted gain/1000 of 34.8, and 95% CI gain of 25.6–44.1.
